part_2_attending_physicians_statement.primary: Acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) involving the left anterior descending (LAD) coronary artery with subsequent ischemic cardiomyopathy and reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (~35%).
part_2_attending_physicians_statement.secondary: Essential hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes mellitus (diet and metformin controlled), obesity (BMI 31), and obstructive sleep apnea on CPAP; all contributing cardiovascular risk factors.
part_2_attending_physicians_statement.frequency_of_visits_other_specify: Initially every 2 weeks for the first 2 months post-MI, transitioning to monthly follow-ups thereafter, with additional unscheduled visits as needed for symptom exacerbations.
part_2_attending_physicians_statement.name_of_hospital: Grand River Hospital – Kitchener-Waterloo Health Centre, Kitchener, Ontario
part_2_attending_physicians_statement.subjective_symptoms_including_severity_f:
The patient reports ongoing exertional chest discomfort, shortness of breath, and significant fatigue since his acute myocardial infarction on 2024-10-29. He describes the chest discomfort as a pressure or tightness located centrally and slightly to the left side of the chest, sometimes radiating to the left shoulder and upper arm. On a 0–10 pain scale, he rates the typical exertional discomfort as 3–4/10, but notes that when he overexerts himself (for example, climbing a full flight of stairs while carrying groceries or walking briskly uphill), it can rise to 6/10, requiring him to stop and rest. These episodes currently occur several times per week, particularly when he attempts household chores, walks more than one to two city blocks at a normal pace, or carries anything over 10–15 pounds.

He experiences dyspnea on exertion that he did not notice prior to his heart attack. He reports that when walking on flat ground, he starts to feel short of breath after approximately 5–10 minutes of steady walking. He needs to pause to catch his breath, especially if he is walking outdoors in cold weather or if he is talking while walking. He also reports that climbing a single flight of stairs leaves him noticeably winded and occasionally lightheaded, and he often needs to rest at the top of the stairs before continuing. He denies resting dyspnea but does report mild orthopnea: he has begun sleeping with two pillows because lying completely flat makes him feel “heavy” in the chest and slightly short of breath. This has been present most nights since shortly after discharge from hospital and is worse on days when he has been more active.

Fatigue is a prominent and persistent symptom. He states that since the heart attack, his overall energy level is significantly reduced compared to his baseline. Prior to 2024-10-29, he worked full-time as an electrical maintenance technician in a manufacturing plant, regularly doing 10,000–12,000 steps per day and working 8–10 hour shifts that included walking the plant floor, climbing ladders, and occasionally lifting equipment or tools weighing 20–40 pounds. Now, even routine daily activities such as showering, dressing, preparing meals, and light housework can leave him feeling exhausted. He reports that by late morning or early afternoon, he often needs to sit or lie down to rest for 30–60 minutes. He describes this fatigue as a combination of physical tiredness and a feeling of being “drained,” with little reserve for any unplanned exertion.

He also reports intermittent palpitations—brief episodes of feeling that his heart is beating faster or more forcefully, particularly when he is anxious or when he first lies down at night. These episodes last from 30 seconds to a few minutes and occur a few times per week. He denies associated syncope but has had a few episodes of mild lightheadedness when standing up quickly from a seated or lying position, which he attributes to his medications and lower blood pressure compared to his pre-MI baseline.

He notes low-grade, diffuse anxiety related to fear of having another heart attack. This anxiety tends to peak when he experiences any chest discomfort or shortness of breath, even if mild, and has led him to be very cautious and somewhat avoidant of physical activity. He frequently checks his pulse and becomes worried when it feels faster than usual, which can exacerbate his sense of breathlessness. He reports poor sleep quality: he awakens multiple times during the night, sometimes due to discomfort from his CPAP mask and sometimes because he is worried about his heart. On average, he estimates he sleeps 4–6 hours per night in fragmented segments.

The duration of these symptoms has been continuous since the acute MI and hospitalization beginning 2024-10-29. The most intense phase was in the first 4–6 weeks after discharge (November through early December 2024) when even minimal exertion (e.g., walking from the living room to the kitchen, showering, or getting dressed) could trigger chest tightness and shortness of breath. Over the last few weeks (mid-December 2024 to early January 2025), there has been some gradual improvement with optimized medical therapy and participation in early cardiac rehabilitation, but he continues to experience reproducible exertional symptoms consistent with New York Heart Association (NYHA) class II–III functional limitation.

In terms of frequency, he experiences some combination of chest pressure, shortness of breath, or marked fatigue on a daily basis. Light activities such as slow indoor walking, standing to cook simple meals, or brief trips to the mailbox are generally tolerated but often followed by increased fatigue. More moderate activities—such as carrying groceries, walking more than 1–2 blocks at a normal pace, climbing a flight of stairs without stopping, or attempting any form of hurried or repetitive physical task—frequently trigger symptoms within minutes and require him to stop, rest, and sometimes use his prescribed sublingual nitroglycerin (which he has used intermittently, approximately 1–2 times per week, with good relief).

He reports that because of these symptoms, he cannot safely perform the essential duties of his previous job, which included prolonged standing and walking, frequent stair and ladder climbing, and handling moderately heavy tools and equipment in a fast-paced industrial environment. He worries that if he attempted to return to that work now, he would quickly develop chest pain and shortness of breath, and he fears potentially collapsing on the job site. He also notes that mental concentration is somewhat impaired by his fatigue and poor sleep, and that he becomes mentally “foggy” by midday, making it difficult to troubleshoot complex electrical issues or focus on detailed safety procedures for prolonged periods.

Overall, his symptoms are moderate to moderately severe, occurring daily with variable intensity, exacerbated by physical and emotional stress, and partially relieved with rest, medication adherence, and pacing of activities. Despite gradual improvement, they have persisted for over two months since the initial cardiac event and remain sufficiently limiting that he is unable at this time to return to his previous physically demanding occupation without significant risk to his health and safety.
